frontmatter.title: Bayesian Regression With Latent Gaussian Sampler

children.value: In this example, we want to illustrate how to use the marginal sampler implementation
. We do so by using the simulated data from the example
Gaussian Regression with the Elliptical Slice Sampler
. Please also refer to the complementary example
Bayesian Logistic Regression With Latent Gaussian Sampler
In section we give a brief overview of the idea behind this particular sampler. For more details please refer to the original paper
you can access the arXiv preprint).
Let us recall how to sample from a target density

children.html: π(x)\pi(\mathbf{x})π(x)

children.value: using a Metropolis-Hasting sampler trough a
. The main idea is to have a mechanism that generate proposals

children.html: yyy

children.value: which we then accept or reject according to a specific criterion. Concretely, suppose that we have an

children.html: u∣x∼π(u∣x)=q(u∣x)\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x} \sim \pi(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x}) = q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x})u∣x∼π(u∣x)=q(u∣x)
y∣u,x∼q(y∣x,u)\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{x} \sim q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})y∣u,x∼q(y∣x,u)

ϱ~=π(y∣u)q(x∣y,u)π(x∣u)q(y∣x,u)\tilde{\varrho} = \frac{\pi(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u})q(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{u})}{\pi(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{u})q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})}ϱ~​=π(x∣u)q(y∣x,u)π(y∣u)q(x∣y,u)​
yyy
min⁡(1,ϱ~)\min(1, \tilde{\varrho})min(1,ϱ~​)

children.value: This scheme targets the auxiliary distribution

children.html: π(x,u)=π(x)q(u∣x)\pi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) = \pi(\mathbf{x}) q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x})π(x,u)=π(x)q(u∣x)

children.value: Now, suppose we can instead compute the

children.html: q(y∣x)=∫q(y∣x,u)q(u∣x)duq(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}) = \int q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x}) \mathrm{d}uq(y∣x)=∫q(y∣x,u)q(u∣x)du

children.value: in closed form, then an alternative scheme is given by:

children.html: y∼q(y∣x)y \sim q(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{x})y∼q(y∣x)

children.value: Then we compute the Metropolis-Hasting ratio

children.html: ϱ=π(y)q(x∣y)π(x)q(y∣x)\varrho = \frac{\pi(\mathbf{y})q(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{y})}{\pi(\mathbf{x})q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x})}ϱ=π(x)q(y∣x)π(y)q(x∣y)​
yyy
min⁡(1,ϱ)\min(1, \varrho)min(1,ϱ)

children.value: Let’s consider the case of an auxiliary random walk proposal

children.html: q(u∣x)=N(u∣x,(δ/2)I)q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x}) = N(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x}, (\delta /2) \mathbf{I})q(u∣x)=N(u∣x,(δ/2)I)
δ>0\delta > 0δ>0

children.value: , it is shown that one can use a first order approximation to sample from the (intractable)

children.html: π(x∣u)\pi(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{u})π(x∣u)
q(y∣u,x)∝N(y∣u+(δ/2)∇log⁡π(x),(δ/2)I).q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{x}) \propto N(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u} + (\delta/2)\nabla \log \pi(\mathbf{x}), (\delta/2) I).q(y∣u,x)∝N(y∣u+(δ/2)∇logπ(x),(δ/2)I).

children.value: The resulting marginal sampler can be shown to correspond to the Metropolis-adjusted Langevin algorithm (MALA) with

children.html: q(y∣x)=N(y∣x+(δ/2)∇log⁡π(x),δI).q(\mathbf{y}| \mathbf{x}) = N(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x} + (\delta/2)\nabla \log \pi(\mathbf{x}), \delta I).q(y∣x)=N(y∣x+(δ/2)∇logπ(x),δI).

children.value: A particular case of interest is the latent Gaussian model where the target density has the form

children.html: π(x)∝exp⁡{f(x)}⏞likelihoodN(x∣0,C)⏟Gaussian Prior\pi(\mathbf{x}) \propto \overbrace{\exp\{f(\mathbf{x})\}}^{\text{likelihood}} \underbrace{N(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{C})}_{\text{Gaussian Prior}}π(x)∝exp{f(x)}​likelihood​Gaussian PriorN(x∣0,C)​​

children.value: In this case, instead of linearising the full log density

children.html: log⁡π(x)\log \pi(\mathbf{x})logπ(x)
fff

children.value: only, which, when combined with a random walk proposal

children.html: N(u∣x,(δ/2)I)N(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{x}, (\delta /2) \mathbf{I})N(u∣x,(δ/2)I)

children.value: , recovers to the following auxiliary proposal

children.html: q(y∣x,u)∝N(y∣2δA(u+δ2∇f(x)),A),q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) \propto N\left(\mathbf{y}|\frac{2}{\delta} \mathbf{A}\left(\mathbf{u} + \frac{\delta}{2}\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\right), \mathbf{A}\right),q(y∣x,u)∝N(y∣δ2​A(u+2δ​∇f(x)),A),
A=δ/2(C+(δ/2)I)−1C\mathbf{A} = \delta / 2(\mathbf{C} + (\delta / 2)\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{C}A=δ/2(C+(δ/2)I)−1C

q(y∣x)∝N(y∣2δA(x+δ2∇f(x)),2δA2+A).q(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{x}) \propto N\left(\mathbf{y}|\frac{2}{\delta} \mathbf{A}\left(\mathbf{x} + \frac{\delta}{2}\nabla f(\mathbf{x})\right), \frac{2}{\delta}\mathbf{A}^2 + \mathbf{A}\right).q(y∣x)∝N(y∣δ2​A(x+2δ​∇f(x)),δ2​A2+A).
π(x,u)\pi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})π(x,u)
π(x)\pi(\mathbf{x})π(x)

children.value: , is done via Hastings-within-Gibbs as above.
A crucial point of this algorithm is the fact that

children.html: A\mathbf{A}A

children.value: can be precomputed and afterward modified cheaply when

children.html: δ\deltaδ

children.value: varies. This makes it easy to calibrate the step-size

children.html: δ\deltaδ

children.value: Now that we have a high-level understanding of the algorithm, let’s see how to use it in

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams["axes.spines.right"] = False
plt.rcParams["axes.spines.top"] = False

import jax

from datetime import date

rng_key = jax.random.key(int(date.today().strftime("%Y%m%d")))

import jax.numpy as jnp
import numpy as np

from blackjax import mgrad_gaussian
We generate data through a squared exponential kernel as in the example
Gaussian Regression with the Elliptical Slice Sampler

n, d = 2000, 2
length, scale = 1.0, 1.0
y_sd = 1.0

# fake data
rng_key, kX, kf, ky = jax.random.split(rng_key, 4)

X = jax.random.uniform(kX, shape=(n, d))
Sigma = jax.vmap(
    lambda x: jax.vmap(lambda y: squared_exponential(x, y, length, scale))(X)
)(X) + 1e-3 * jnp.eye(n)
invSigma = jnp.linalg.inv(Sigma)
f = jax.random.multivariate_normal(kf, jnp.zeros(n), Sigma)
y = f + jax.random.normal(ky, shape=(n,)) * y_sd

# conjugate results
posterior_cov = jnp.linalg.inv(invSigma + 1 / y_sd**2 * jnp.eye(n))
posterior_mean = jnp.dot(posterior_cov, y) * 1 / y_sd**2
Let’s visualize the distribution of the vector

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(np.array(y), bins=50, density=True)
plt.xlabel("y")
plt.title("Histogram of data.")
plt.show()
Now we proceed to run the sampler. First, we set the sampler parameters:
Next, we define the the log-probability function. For this we need to set the log-likelihood function.

loglikelihood_fn = lambda f: -0.5 * jnp.dot(y - f, y - f) / y_sd**2
logdensity_fn = lambda f: loglikelihood_fn(f) - 0.5 * jnp.dot(f @ invSigma, f)
Now we are ready to initialize the sampler. The output is type is a

init:
    A pure function which when called with the initial position and the
    target density probability function will return the kernel's initial
    state.

step:
    A pure function that takes a rng key, a state and possibly some
    parameters and returns a new state and some information about the
    transition.
We continue by setting the inference loop.
We are now ready to run the sampler! The only extra parameters in the
, which (as seen in the sampler description) corresponds (in a loose sense) to the step-size of MALA algorithm.
Note that one can calibrate the
parameter as described in the example
Bayesian Logistic Regression With Latent Gaussian Sampler

%%time

initial_state = init(f)
rng_key, sample_key = jax.random.split(rng_key, 2)
states, info = inference_loop(sample_key, init(f), kernel, n_warm + n_iter)
samples = states.position[n_warm:]

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
CPU times: user 19.8 s, sys: 147 ms, total: 19.9 s
Wall time: 9.46 s

children.value:
error_mean = jnp.mean((samples.mean(axis=0) - posterior_mean) ** 2)
error_cov = jnp.mean((jnp.cov(samples, rowvar=False) - posterior_cov) ** 2)
print(
    f"Mean squared error for the mean vector {error_mean} and covariance matrix {error_cov}"
)

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: Mean squared error for the mean vector 0.002274061320349574 and covariance matrix 1.3194022585594212e-06

children.value:
rng_key, sample_key = jax.random.split(rng_key, 2)
keys = jax.random.split(sample_key, 500)
predictive = jax.vmap(lambda k, f: f + jax.random.normal(k, (n,)) * y_sd)(
    keys, samples[-1000:]
)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(np.array(y), bins=50, density=True)
plt.hist(np.array(predictive.reshape(-1)), bins=50, density=True, alpha=0.8)
plt.xlabel("y")
plt.title("Predictive distribution")
plt.show()
